content.assessment: This proposal is explicitly non-structural and sequences investment prior to any structural change, which the lens treats as a distinct category from Poland's structural interventions. The Polish evidence provides no direct analogue to a compensatory quality-investment strategy that preserves existing tiers, so portability of Polish outcome data is minimal here. Still, the lens's distributional-effects criterion is relevant: Poland's 1999 reform gains were concentrated among students who would otherwise have been tracked into lower-status pathways, implying that the underlying deficit addressed was structural exclusion rather than resource scarcity alone. This raises a genuine tension for the proposal: if demand for selective tracks in the Hungarian case is partly status-driven rather than purely quality-driven, compensatory investment alone may not replicate the structural redistribution effect documented in Poland.
opportunities: Could be sequenced as a preparatory phase preceding eventual structural reform, similar to how Poland's structural reform required years before full outcome data (PISA 2022) confirmed effects, suggesting long investment horizons may be necessary regardless of strategy [F-live-polish_reform-07]
strengths: Avoids the severe implementation costs and reversibility risk documented in Poland's structural reversal, since no tier abolition or school closure is proposed [F-live-polish_reform-05]
threats: Preserving current tiers without structural change risks replicating the pre-1999 Polish stratification pattern that the single-structure reform was specifically designed to address
weaknesses: Does not engage the structural mechanism (entry-point tracking) that Polish evidence associates with measurable distributional gains, leaving the core selection funnel intact [F-live-polish_reform-04]
No Polish precedent exists for a preserve-tracks-and-invest strategy, so the lens offers no directly comparable implementation-sequencing evidence
